1:
"I say the truth in Christ, I lie not, my conscience also bearing me witness in the Holy Ghost," The witnesses note Paul's solemn protestation of sincerity. On "I say the truth in Christ, I lie not," the grammatical voices note the oath-like affirmation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul solemnly asserts his truthfulness — in Christ (as a Christian, before Christ) he does not lie, a strong protestation introducing his grief for Israel (lest the Jews think he had no love for his nation).

On "my conscience bearing witness in the Holy Ghost," the witnesses note the inward testimony. Paul's conscience, enlightened by the Holy Spirit, joins in attesting the truth of his sorrow — a double witness, his own word and his conscience.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the purpose: Paul, about to express deep grief for the Jews who opposed him, guards against the charge of insincerity by this solemn affirmation. The verse displays Paul's solemn protestation of his sincere grief.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul solemnly affirms his truthfulness — in Christ he does not lie, his conscience bearing witness in the Holy Spirit (Barnes, Robertson) — a strong protestation guarding against the charge of insincerity, lest the Jews think he had no love for his nation (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's solemn protestation. The verse marks the affirmation: the truth in Christ. The verse displays the witness: his conscience in the Spirit. The verse displays the purpose: introducing his grief. The verse displays the sincerity: guarded by oath. The protestation displays the depth and genuineness of Paul's love for Israel. The verse displays the truth: in Christ, no lie. The verse displays the double witness: word and conscience. The verse displays the introduction: to his great sorrow. Paul's solemn protestation of his sincere grief — speaking the truth in Christ, his conscience bearing witness in the Holy Spirit — displays the depth and genuineness of his love for Israel: about to express profound sorrow for his kinsmen who had rejected the gospel, he guards against any charge of insincerity by this oath-like affirmation. The verse displays the apostle's tender heart for his people, and introduces the great heaviness he bore for them (v.2).
2:
"That I have great heaviness and continual sorrow in my heart." The brief verse notes Paul's great sorrow for Israel. The grammatical voices note the intensity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul confesses "great heaviness" (grief, pain) and "continual sorrow" (unceasing anguish) in his heart — a deep, abiding grief for his people.

On the cause, the witnesses note the object. The sorrow is for unbelieving Israel — his kinsmen who had rejected the Messiah and were thereby cut off; their lostness weighs perpetually on the apostle.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the love: Paul's grief proves his unfeigned affection for his nation, contradicting the charge that he was their enemy; he sorrows even for those who persecuted him. The verse displays Paul's great and continual sorrow for Israel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul confesses great heaviness (grief) and continual sorrow (unceasing anguish) in his heart (Barnes, Robertson) — a deep, abiding grief for unbelieving Israel, his kinsmen cut off by their rejection of the Messiah. Calvin and Theodoret note the love: Paul's grief proves his unfeigned affection for his nation, even for those who persecuted him. The verse displays Paul's great sorrow. The verse marks the intensity: great heaviness. The verse displays the continuance: continual sorrow. The verse displays the cause: Israel's lostness. The verse displays the love: for his persecutors. The sorrow displays the depth of Paul's love for his lost people. The verse displays the grief: weighing on his heart. The verse displays the perpetuity: unceasing. The verse displays the object: unbelieving Israel. Paul's great and continual sorrow for Israel displays the depth of his love for his lost people: the apostle bore a perpetual, heart-deep grief for his kinsmen who had rejected the Messiah, sorrowing even for those who opposed him. The verse displays the unfeigned affection that contradicts any charge of enmity, and leads to the astonishing expression of his love (v.3). This perpetual ache of heart, borne for a people that for the most part returned him only hatred, is the surest token that the apostle wrote not as their adversary but as one who would gladly have spent himself for their souls.
3:
"For I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh:" The witnesses note Paul's astonishing wish of love. On "I could wish myself accursed from Christ," the grammatical voices note the force. Barnes, Robertson, and Clarke note the intensity of love: Paul could (almost) wish himself accursed (anathema, devoted to destruction, separated from Christ) for his brethren — the strongest possible expression of self-sacrificing love.

On the meaning, the witnesses debate. Most note this is not a fixed resolve (he knew it impossible and unlawful to be finally separated from Christ) but the language of overflowing love — a wish he checks ("I could wish"), expressing how intensely he loved his people, like Moses (Exodus 32:32).

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the parallel with Moses: such love seeks the good of others above its own, willing even the greatest loss for their salvation; yet the wish is governed by submission to God. The verse displays Paul's astonishing wish of self-sacrificing love.

The witnesses agree the verse expresses the strongest possible love: Paul could (almost) wish himself accursed (anathema, devoted to destruction, separated from Christ) for his brethren, his kinsmen (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke). Most note this is not a fixed resolve (he knew final separation from Christ impossible and unlawful) but the language of overflowing love — a wish he checks, like Moses (Exodus 32:32). Calvin and Theodoret note the parallel with Moses: such love seeks others' good above its own, yet governed by submission to God. The verse displays Paul's astonishing wish. The verse marks the intensity: accursed from Christ. The verse displays the object: his brethren. The verse displays the meaning: overflowing love, not fixed resolve. The verse displays the parallel: Moses. The wish displays the boundless self-sacrificing love of the apostle. The verse displays the love: willing the greatest loss. The verse displays the restraint: a wish checked. The verse displays the model: Moses's intercession. Paul's astonishing wish — that he could be accursed from Christ for his brethren — displays the boundless, self-sacrificing love of the apostle for his people: not a fixed and lawful resolve, but the overflowing language of a love that, like Moses, would bear the greatest loss for their salvation. The verse displays the depth of Paul's affection for Israel, and lists the privileges that made their unbelief so grievous (vv.4-5).
4:
"Who are Israelites; to whom pertaineth the adoption, and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law, and the service of God, and the promises;" The witnesses note the privileges of Israel. On "Israelites," the grammatical voices note the honored name. Barnes and Robertson note "Israelites" is the covenant name, marking their descent from Jacob (Israel) and their place as God's chosen nation.

On the list of privileges, the witnesses note the rich heritage. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the privileges: the adoption (Israel as God's son, Exodus 4:22), the glory (the Shekinah, God's manifested presence), the covenants (with Abraham, Moses, David), the giving of the law (at Sinai), the service of God (the temple worship), and the promises (of the Messiah and the kingdom).

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the purpose: Paul recounts these privileges to magnify the grief of their unbelief — that a nation so favored should reject the Messiah. The verse displays the rich privileges of Israel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the "Israelites" (the covenant name, marking descent from Jacob) possessed rich privileges — the adoption (Israel God's son), the glory (the Shekinah presence), the covenants (Abraham, Moses, David), the giving of the law (at Sinai), the service of God (the temple worship), and the promises (of the Messiah, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Calvin and Theodoret note Paul recounts these to magnify the grief of their unbelief. The verse displays the privileges of Israel. The verse marks the name: Israelites. The verse displays the heritage: adoption, glory, covenants, law, service, promises. The verse displays the favor: a nation richly blessed. The verse displays the purpose: magnifying their unbelief. The privileges display the greatness of Israel's heritage. The verse displays the adoption: God's son. The verse displays the glory: the divine presence. The verse displays the covenants and promises. The rich privileges of Israel — the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the law, the service, and the promises — display the greatness of the heritage God bestowed on his chosen nation. The verse displays the favor that made their rejection of the Messiah so grievous, the apostle recounting their blessings to heighten the tragedy of their unbelief, and crowning the list with the supreme privilege (v.5).
5:
"Whose are the fathers, and of whom as concerning the flesh Christ came, who is over all, God blessed for ever. Amen." The witnesses note the supreme privilege and the deity of Christ. On "whose are the fathers... of whom Christ came," the grammatical voices note the crowning honor. Barnes and Robertson note the highest privilege: Israel had the patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) as fathers, and from them, according to the flesh (his human nature), came Christ — the Messiah, the hope of the world.

On "who is over all, God blessed for ever," the witnesses note the deity of Christ — and the punctuation debate. Most (Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley) affirm this declares Christ's deity: he who came in the flesh is "over all, God blessed forever" — a clear assertion of his divine nature, the natural reading. Ellicott notes the alternative (a doxology to the Father, the Socinian reading) but, weighing both, leans to the application to Christ.

The witnesses note the force: "according to the flesh" implies a higher nature; the doxology and the title "God" belong to Christ, the two natures distinguished yet united. The verse displays Christ of Israel's fathers according to the flesh, yet over all, God blessed forever.

The witnesses agree the crowning privilege was that from Israel's fathers, according to the flesh, came Christ — and that the clause "who is over all, God blessed for ever" declares his deity (Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley): he who came in the flesh is the eternal God, the natural reading, with "according to the flesh" implying a higher nature. Ellicott notes the alternative doxology-to-the-Father reading (the Socinian view) but, weighing the arguments, leans to the application to Christ. The verse displays the deity of Christ. The verse marks the privilege: Christ of the fathers. The verse displays the human nature: according to the flesh. The verse displays the deity: over all, God blessed forever. The verse displays the debate: of Christ, or a doxology. The verse displays the two natures: united in Christ. The supreme privilege displays Christ's descent from Israel and his eternal deity. The verse displays the honor: the Messiah from the fathers. The verse displays the Godhead: blessed forever. The verse displays the affirmation: Christ over all. The supreme privilege and the deity of Christ — that from Israel's fathers, according to the flesh, came Christ, who is over all, God blessed forever — display both Israel's crowning honor and one of the clearest assertions of Christ's deity in Scripture: he who took flesh from Israel is the eternal God, the two natures distinguished yet united in his one person. The verse displays the divine glory of the Messiah, against which the punctuation objections cannot prevail, and turns to the apparent failure of God's word (v.6).
6:
"Not as though the word of God hath taken none effect. For they are not all Israel, which are of Israel:" The witnesses note that God's word has not failed, for the true Israel is the remnant of promise. On "the word of God hath not taken none effect," the grammatical voices note the defense. Barnes, Henry, and Robertson note Paul anticipates the objection: if Israel is rejected, has God's promise failed? No — the word of God has not fallen or failed.

On "they are not all Israel, which are of Israel," the witnesses note the key distinction. Barnes and Calvin note the crucial principle: not all who are physically descended from Israel (Jacob) are the true Israel; there is a spiritual Israel within the natural — the children of promise, not merely of the flesh.

The Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the resolution: God's promise was never to all the natural seed indiscriminately, but to the elect, the children of promise; so the rejection of unbelieving Israel does not nullify the word. The verse displays the distinction: the true Israel is the spiritual seed, not all the natural.

The witnesses agree: God's word has not failed (despite Israel's rejection, Barnes, Henry, Robertson), for the crucial distinction holds — not all physically descended from Israel are the true Israel; there is a spiritual Israel within the natural, the children of promise, not merely of the flesh (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Henry note the resolution: God's promise was never to all the natural seed indiscriminately but to the elect, the children of promise, so the rejection of unbelieving Israel does not nullify the word. The verse displays the distinction. The verse marks the defense: God's word not failed. The verse displays the principle: not all Israel are Israel. The verse displays the spiritual seed: the children of promise. The verse displays the resolution: the promise to the elect. The distinction displays the true Israel as the spiritual, not merely natural, seed. The verse displays the defense: God's word stands. The verse displays the key: a spiritual Israel within. The verse displays the promise: to the children of promise. That God's word has not failed, for they are not all Israel who are of Israel, displays the crucial distinction governing the whole chapter: the true Israel is the spiritual seed, the children of promise, not all the natural descendants. The verse displays that God's promise was never to the whole natural nation indiscriminately but to the elect, so that the rejection of unbelieving Israel leaves the divine word unshaken. The verse establishes the principle of election that Paul illustrates from Isaac and Jacob (vv.7-13).
7:
"Neither, because they are the seed of Abraham, are they all children: but, In Isaac shall thy seed be called." The witnesses note the seed of promise, not merely of the flesh. On "not all children because Abraham's seed," the grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Calvin note that mere physical descent from Abraham does not make one a true child of the promise — Ishmael was Abraham's seed but not the child of promise.

On "in Isaac shall thy seed be called," the witnesses note the divine choice. Gill and Barnes note God's word (Genesis 21:12) — the line of promise runs through Isaac, not Ishmael; the seed is reckoned through the child of promise, by God's election.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the principle: the true seed of Abraham is constituted by God's promise and choice, not by natural generation alone. The verse displays the seed of promise reckoned through Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: mere physical descent from Abraham does not make one a true child of the promise (Ishmael was Abraham's seed but not the child of promise, Barnes, Calvin); for God said "in Isaac shall thy seed be called" (Genesis 21:12) — the line of promise running through Isaac, not Ishmael, by God's election (Gill, Barnes). Cyril and Calvin note the true seed is constituted by God's promise and choice, not natural generation alone. The verse displays the seed of promise. The verse marks the distinction: not all Abraham's seed are children. The verse displays the choice: in Isaac the seed called. The verse displays the principle: promise, not flesh. The verse displays the election: God's reckoning. The seed of promise displays the divine choice constituting the true children. The verse displays the exclusion: Ishmael not the child of promise. The verse displays the line: through Isaac. The verse displays the basis: God's word. The seed of promise reckoned through Isaac, not all Abraham's natural seed, displays the divine choice that constitutes the true children of God: it is not natural descent but God's promise and election that determine who is reckoned the seed. The verse displays the first illustration of the principle that the true Israel is the seed of promise, distinguishing Isaac from Ishmael, and explains the nature of the children of promise (vv.8-9).
8:
"That is, They which are the children of the flesh, these are not the children of God: but the children of the promise are counted for the seed." The witnesses note the children of promise, not of the flesh. On "children of the flesh are not children of God," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul interprets the Isaac/Ishmael distinction: mere natural descent (children of the flesh) does not constitute one a child of God.

On "the children of the promise counted for the seed," the witnesses note the true seed. The children of promise — those born by virtue of God's promise (as Isaac), and so the spiritual seed — are reckoned (counted) as the true seed of Abraham.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the application: the true children of God are not the merely natural Israel but those constituted by promise (and, for the gospel, by faith); the principle distinguishing the spiritual from the carnal seed. The verse displays the children of promise counted for the seed.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul interprets the Isaac/Ishmael distinction — mere natural descent (children of the flesh) does not constitute one a child of God (Barnes, Robertson); rather, the children of promise (those born by virtue of God's promise, as Isaac, the spiritual seed) are counted as the true seed of Abraham. Calvin and Wesley note the true children of God are not the merely natural Israel but those constituted by promise (and, in the gospel, by faith). The verse displays the children of promise. The verse marks the principle: not the children of the flesh. The verse displays the true seed: the children of promise. The verse displays the reckoning: counted for the seed. The verse displays the application: spiritual vs carnal seed. The children of promise display the spiritual constitution of the true seed. The verse displays the exclusion: the flesh not enough. The verse displays the promise: the basis of sonship. The verse displays the counting: as the true seed. The children of promise counted for the seed, not the children of the flesh, display the spiritual constitution of the true people of God: it is not natural descent but God's promise that makes one a child of God and the true seed of Abraham. The verse displays the principle distinguishing the spiritual Israel from the merely carnal, and cites the promise that made Isaac the child of promise (v.9).
9:
"For this is the word of promise, At this time will I come, and Sarah shall have a son." The brief verse notes the word of promise that made Isaac the child of promise. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes God's promise (Genesis 18:10, 14): at the appointed time God would come and Sarah would have a son — Isaac born not by the course of nature but by the power of God's promise.

On the point, the witnesses note the supernatural birth. Isaac's birth was the fruit of God's promise (Sarah being past childbearing) — proving that the true seed is constituted by promise and divine power, not by natural generation.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: Isaac, the child of promise, is the type of all who are God's children by promise and grace, not by the flesh. The verse displays the word of promise that gave Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes God's promise (Genesis 18:10, 14) — at the appointed time God would come and Sarah would have a son (Barnes, Robertson) — Isaac born not by the course of nature (Sarah past childbearing) but by the power of God's promise. Calvin notes Isaac, the child of promise, is the type of all who are God's children by promise and grace, not the flesh. The verse displays the word of promise. The verse marks the citation: the promise of Isaac. The verse displays the supernatural birth: by promise, not nature. The verse displays the power: God's word fulfilled. The verse displays the type: Isaac the child of promise. The word of promise displays the divine power constituting the true seed. The verse displays the promise: Sarah shall have a son. The verse displays the miracle: birth beyond nature. The verse displays the principle: the seed by promise. The word of promise that gave Isaac — "at this time will I come, and Sarah shall have a son" — displays the divine power that constitutes the true seed: Isaac was born not by the course of nature but by the fulfillment of God's promise, the type of all who are God's children by promise and grace rather than by the flesh. The verse displays the supernatural ground of the true sonship, and turns to a yet clearer case of election in Jacob and Esau (vv.10-13).
10:
"And not only this; but when Rebecca also had conceived by one, even by our father Isaac;" The witnesses note the clearer case of Jacob and Esau. On "not only this," the grammatical voices note the stronger example. Barnes and Robertson note Paul advances to a case even clearer than Isaac/Ishmael: Jacob and Esau, who had not only the same father (Isaac) but the same mother (Rebecca), and were conceived at the same time — twins.

On "conceived by one, even by Isaac," the witnesses note the force. Unlike Ishmael (son of the bondwoman) and Isaac (son of the free), Jacob and Esau were full brothers, twins of the same parents — so the distinction between them could rest on nothing but God's choice.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the purpose: this case removes every ground of distinction but God's election (or, for some, God's foreknowledge); the twins being equal in descent. The verse displays the clearer case: Jacob and Esau, twins of one father and mother.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul advances to a case clearer than Isaac/Ishmael — Jacob and Esau, who had the same father (Isaac) and mother (Rebecca) and were conceived at the same time, twins (Barnes, Robertson); unlike Ishmael and Isaac (different mothers), the twins were full brothers, so the distinction between them could rest on nothing but God's choice. Calvin and Wesley note this removes every ground but God's election (or, for some, foreknowledge). The verse displays the clearer case. The verse marks the advance: not only this. The verse displays the twins: one father and mother. The verse displays the force: equal in descent. The verse displays the purpose: distinction by choice alone. The clearer case displays the removal of every ground but God's choice. The verse displays the equality: full brothers. The verse displays the conception: by one father. The verse displays the setup: for election. The clearer case of Jacob and Esau — twins conceived by one father and mother — displays the removal of every ground of distinction but God's choice: unlike Isaac and Ishmael, the twins were wholly equal in descent, so that the difference between them could rest only on God's election. The verse displays the strongest illustration of the sovereign principle, and states the divine purpose behind the choice (vv.11-13).
11:
"(For the children being not yet born, neither having done any good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works, but of him that calleth;)" The witnesses note the purpose of God according to election — the central crux. On "not yet born, neither having done good or evil," the grammatical voices note the timing. Barnes and Robertson note the choice between Jacob and Esau was made before they were born or had done anything good or evil — so it could not rest on their works or character.

On "the purpose of God according to election might stand," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes notes the choice was according to God's purpose and election, not the merits of the individuals — God's sovereign plan, lying back of any works.

On "not of works, but of him that calleth," the great debate divides. The Reformed Calvin and Gill hold this teaches unconditional individual election (and reprobation): God chose Jacob and rejected Esau by his free will alone, not from foreseen works — refuting the notion of election by foreseen merit. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke holds the election is national (to outward privilege, Israel over Edom), not personal eternal salvation; Barnes affirms sovereign election not of works but stops short of unconditional reprobation. The verse displays the purpose of God according to election, not of works.

The witnesses agree the choice between Jacob and Esau was made before they were born or had done good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works but of him that calleth (Barnes, Robertson) — so it could not rest on their merits. But the great debate divides on its scope. The Reformed Calvin and Gill hold unconditional individual election and reprobation — God chose Jacob and rejected Esau by his free will alone, not from foreseen works (refuting election by foreseen merit). The wesleyan-arminian Clarke holds the election is national (to outward privilege, not personal eternal salvation); Barnes affirms sovereign election not of works. The verse displays the purpose of God according to election. The verse marks the timing: before birth or works. The verse displays the ground: God's purpose, not works. The verse displays the debate: individual or national, conditional or not. The verse displays the calling: of him that calleth. The purpose of God according to election displays the sovereign ground of the divine choice. The verse displays the freedom: not of works. The verse displays the crux: the scope of election. The verse displays the source: him that calleth. The purpose of God according to election, not of works but of him that calleth, displays the sovereign ground of the divine choice — made before the twins were born or had done good or evil — and opens the central crux of the chapter: whether this teaches the unconditional individual election and reprobation of the Reformed, or the national election to privilege of the Arminian. The verse displays the foundation of the doctrine of election, that it rests on God's free purpose and not on human works, and is confirmed by the word to Rebecca (vv.12-13).
12:
"It was said unto her, The elder shall serve the younger." The brief verse notes the word to Rebecca: the elder shall serve the younger. On "it was said unto her," the grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note God's word to Rebecca (Genesis 25:23) — contrary to the natural order of primogeniture, the elder (Esau) should serve the younger (Jacob).

On the reference, the witnesses note the nations. Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley note this refers primarily to the posterity — the descendants of Esau (Edom) would be subject to the descendants of Jacob (Israel); the prophecy fulfilled in the nations, not in the persons (Jacob never personally ruled Esau).

The schools read it by their view. The Reformed Calvin holds the temporal dominion symbolized the deeper election; the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley stress it is national and temporal, not personal eternal reprobation. The verse displays the word to Rebecca: the elder to serve the younger.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God's word to Rebecca (Genesis 25:23) reversed the natural order — the elder (Esau) should serve the younger (Jacob) — referring primarily to the posterity: Edom subject to Israel, the prophecy fulfilled in the nations (Jacob never personally ruled Esau, Barnes, Clarke, Wesley). The schools read it variously: Calvin holds the temporal dominion symbolized the deeper election; Clarke and Wesley stress it is national and temporal, not personal eternal reprobation. The verse displays the word to Rebecca. The verse marks the citation: the elder shall serve the younger. The verse displays the reversal: against primogeniture. The verse displays the reference: the nations. The verse displays the reading: national or symbolic. The word to Rebecca displays the divine choice reversing the natural order. The verse displays the prophecy: Edom to serve Israel. The verse displays the fulfillment: in the nations. The verse displays the debate: temporal or eternal. The word to Rebecca — the elder shall serve the younger — displays the divine choice reversing the natural order of primogeniture: spoken primarily of the nations (Edom subject to Israel), it is read by the Reformed as symbolizing the deeper election and by the Arminian as national and temporal, not personal eternal reprobation. The verse displays the prophetic word that grounded God's choice of Jacob, and is confirmed by Malachi's "Jacob have I loved, Esau have I hated" (v.13).
13:
"As it is written, Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated." The witnesses note the confirming word: Jacob loved, Esau hated. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note this is from Malachi 1:2-3, spoken long after, confirming the choice — God preferred Jacob (and his posterity, Israel) and rejected Esau (and his posterity, Edom).

On "hated," the witnesses note the comparative sense. Barnes and Robertson note "hated" here does not mean positive hatred but comparative — less loved, or the withholding of favour; as in the Hebrew idiom (Genesis 29:30-31; Luke 14:26, "hate father and mother"). God loved Jacob and loved Esau less.

The great debate divides. The Reformed Calvin and Gill hold this confirms election and reprobation — God's free love to Jacob and rejection of Esau. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley insist it is national (Israel and Edom), not personal eternal states; Malachi speaks of the nations' temporal fortunes, not individual damnation. The verse displays the confirming word: Jacob loved, Esau hated.

The witnesses agree the citation (Malachi 1:2-3, spoken long after) confirms the choice — God preferred Jacob (and Israel) and rejected Esau (and Edom) — with "hated" in the comparative sense (not positive hatred but less loved, the Hebrew idiom, Genesis 29:30-31; Luke 14:26, Barnes, Robertson). The great debate divides: the Reformed Calvin and Gill hold it confirms election and reprobation (God's free love and rejection); the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley insist it is national (Israel and Edom), not personal eternal states, Malachi speaking of the nations' temporal fortunes. The verse displays the confirming word. The verse marks the citation: Malachi. The verse displays the love: to Jacob. The verse displays the "hatred": comparative, less loved. The verse displays the debate: individual or national. The confirming word displays the divine preference and its disputed scope. The verse displays the love: free and sovereign. The verse displays the rejection: of Esau. The verse displays the crux: eternal or national. The confirming word — Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated — displays the divine preference, with "hated" in the comparative sense of less loved or favour withheld, and sustains the central debate: the Reformed see God's sovereign electing love and reprobation, the Arminian a national distinction between Israel and Edom in their temporal fortunes, not individual eternal states. The verse displays the confirming prophecy and the heart of the controversy, leading to the objection that this charges God with unrighteousness (v.14).
14:
"What shall we say then? Is there unrighteousness with God? God forbid." The witnesses note the objection of injustice rejected. On "is there unrighteousness with God?" the grammatical voices note the objection. Barnes, Henry, and Robertson note Paul anticipates the natural objection: if God chooses sovereignly, electing one and rejecting another, is he not unjust? This is the standing objection to the doctrine of sovereignty.

On "God forbid," the witnesses note the emphatic rejection. Robertson notes the vehement denial (me genoito): there is no unrighteousness with God; his sovereignty is not injustice.

The Reformed Calvin notes the flesh storms at the doctrine; Paul does not retreat but defends God's righteousness (vv.15-18). The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes God's justice is vindicated because he deals with men according to his revealed terms. The verse displays the objection of injustice rejected.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the natural objection — if God chooses sovereignly, electing one and rejecting another, is he not unjust? (the standing objection to sovereignty, Barnes, Henry, Robertson) — and vehemently rejects it ("God forbid," me genoito): there is no unrighteousness with God. Calvin notes the flesh storms at the doctrine but Paul defends God's righteousness; Wesley notes God's justice is vindicated by his dealing according to his revealed terms. The verse displays the objection rejected. The verse marks the objection: unrighteousness with God? The verse displays the rejection: God forbid. The verse displays the issue: sovereignty and justice. The verse displays the defense to come: vv.15-18. The objection rejected displays the vindication of God's righteousness in his sovereignty. The verse displays the cavil: God unjust? The verse displays the denial: no unrighteousness. The verse displays the doctrine: sovereignty defended. The objection of injustice rejected — is there unrighteousness with God? God forbid — displays the vindication of God's righteousness in his sovereign election: Paul faces the standing objection that the doctrine of sovereignty makes God unjust, and emphatically denies it. The verse displays the apostle's refusal to retreat from the doctrine, and his readiness to defend the divine righteousness, which he proceeds to do from the Scriptures (vv.15-18).
15:
"For he saith to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I will have compassion." The witnesses note God's sovereign prerogative of mercy. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes God's word to Moses (Exodus 33:19): God claims the absolute right to bestow mercy and compassion on whom he will — the sovereign prerogative of grace.

On the meaning, the witnesses note the freedom of mercy. Mercy is, by its nature, free and undeserved; God owes it to none, and may bestow it according to his own will, not by any claim or merit of man.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley read it by their view: Calvin sees the absolute sovereignty of electing grace; Wesley sees God's freedom to fix the terms of mercy (mercy to believers). The verse displays God's sovereign prerogative to show mercy on whom he will.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes God's word to Moses (Exodus 33:19) — God claims the absolute right to bestow mercy and compassion on whom he will (Barnes, Robertson); mercy being by nature free and undeserved, owed to none, bestowed according to God's own will, not man's merit. The schools read it variously: Calvin sees the absolute sovereignty of electing grace; Wesley sees God's freedom to fix the terms of mercy (mercy to believers). The verse displays God's sovereign prerogative of mercy. The verse marks the citation: the word to Moses. The verse displays the prerogative: mercy on whom he will. The verse displays the freedom: mercy undeserved. The verse displays the reading: sovereign election or revealed terms. God's sovereign prerogative displays the freedom of mercy. The verse displays the right: to show mercy freely. The verse displays the nature: mercy undeserved. The verse displays the will: God's own choice. God's sovereign prerogative to show mercy on whom he will displays the freedom of divine grace: mercy is by its nature free and undeserved, owed to none, and God bestows it according to his own sovereign will, not by any claim or merit of man. The verse displays the vindication of God's righteousness — that since mercy is free, God does no wrong in bestowing it as he pleases — and draws the conclusion that salvation is not of human willing or running (v.16).
16:
"So then it is not of him that willeth, nor of him that runneth, but of God that sheweth mercy." The witnesses note the conclusion: salvation of God's mercy, not man's will or works. On "not of him that willeth, nor of him that runneth," the grammatical voices note the exclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul concludes that mercy (salvation) does not depend on man's will (desire, resolve) nor his running (effort, striving) but on God who shows mercy — excluding human merit as the ground.

On "but of God that sheweth mercy," the witnesses note the source. The whole depends on God's free mercy, not on anything in man — the ground of salvation is in God alone.

The schools read it by their view. The Reformed Calvin holds this teaches that election and salvation depend wholly on God's mercy, not human will or effort; the wesleyan-arminian Wesley holds it means salvation is not by man's will or works as meritorious, but by God's mercy received by faith. The verse displays the conclusion: salvation of God's mercy, not man's will or running.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes that mercy (salvation) does not depend on man's will (desire) nor his running (effort) but on God who shows mercy (Barnes, Robertson) — excluding human merit as the ground; the whole depending on God's free mercy. The schools read it variously: Calvin holds election and salvation depend wholly on God's mercy, not human will or effort; Wesley holds salvation is not by man's will or works as meritorious but by God's mercy received by faith. The verse displays the conclusion. The verse marks the exclusion: not will or running. The verse displays the source: God who shows mercy. The verse displays the ground: in God alone. The verse displays the reading: wholly of God, or not by merit. The conclusion displays the dependence of salvation on God's mercy. The verse displays the negation: not man's will. The verse displays the negation: not man's effort. The verse displays the affirmation: God's mercy. The conclusion that salvation is not of him that willeth nor of him that runneth, but of God that sheweth mercy, displays the dependence of salvation wholly on the divine mercy: it does not rest on human desire or effort as its ground, but on God's free compassion. The verse displays the great principle that excludes all boasting and casts the sinner wholly on God's mercy, read by the Reformed as the sovereignty of electing grace and by the Arminian as the exclusion of human merit, and is illustrated by the case of Pharaoh (vv.17-18).
17:
"For the scripture saith unto Pharaoh, Even for this same purpose have I raised thee up, that I might shew my power in thee, and that my name might be declared throughout all the earth." The witnesses note the case of Pharaoh: raised up to show God's power. On "raised thee up," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Barnes and Robertson note God's word to Pharaoh (Exodus 9:16): "raised up" means brought onto the stage of history, sustained, and made the occasion for displaying God's power — not necessarily created for destruction, but raised to this place and used for God's purpose.

On "that I might shew my power... my name declared," the witnesses note the design. God's purpose in Pharaoh was to manifest his power (in the plagues and the Exodus) and to spread his name throughout the earth — Pharaoh's obstinacy made the occasion of God's glory.

The schools read it by their view. The Reformed Calvin holds God ordained Pharaoh for this end; the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke hold God raised up Pharaoh (already wicked) and used his self-hardening to display his power, not decreeing his sin. The verse displays Pharaoh raised up to show God's power and spread his name.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God's word to Pharaoh (Exodus 9:16) — "raised thee up" means brought onto history's stage and made the occasion for displaying God's power (Barnes, Robertson) — that God might show his power (in the plagues and Exodus) and spread his name throughout the earth, Pharaoh's obstinacy made the occasion of God's glory. The schools read it variously: Calvin holds God ordained Pharaoh for this end; Wesley and Clarke hold God raised up the already-wicked Pharaoh and used his self-hardening to display his power, not decreeing his sin. The verse displays Pharaoh raised up. The verse marks the meaning: raised onto history's stage. The verse displays the design: to show God's power. The verse displays the end: God's name declared. The verse displays the reading: ordained or used. The case of Pharaoh displays God's sovereign use of the wicked for his glory. The verse displays the raising: to this place. The verse displays the power: shown in him. The verse displays the name: spread abroad. The case of Pharaoh, raised up to show God's power and spread his name, displays God's sovereign use of even the wicked for his glory: Pharaoh's obstinacy became the occasion of the mighty displays of the Exodus and the spread of God's name. The verse displays the divine purpose served even by the rebellious, read by the Reformed as God's ordination and by the Arminian as God's use of the self-hardened Pharaoh, and draws the two-fold conclusion of mercy and hardening (v.18).
18:
"Therefore hath he mercy on whom he will have mercy, and whom he will he hardeneth." The witnesses note the two-fold conclusion: mercy and hardening — and the hardening crux. On "mercy on whom he will," the grammatical voices note the first part. Barnes and Robertson note the conclusion: God shows mercy sovereignly, on whom he will.

On "whom he will he hardeneth," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott) and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley read the hardening as judicial and permissive: God does not exert a positive influence to make men sinful, but leaves the sinner to his own course, withdrawing restraint (Pharaoh hardened his own heart too, Exodus 8:15, 32); a giving-up (as 1:24). The Reformed Calvin reads it as more than permission — the operation of God's wrath, God ordaining and using even Satan and external means to blind the reprobate, though not as the author of their sin.

The witnesses note the limit: Paul forbids us to go higher than God's will; the mystery is to be adored. The verse displays the two-fold conclusion: mercy and hardening, with the nature of the hardening disputed.

The witnesses agree the conclusion is two-fold: God shows mercy on whom he will and hardens whom he will (Barnes, Robertson) — but divide on the hardening. The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott) and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley read it as judicial and permissive: God exerts no positive influence to make men sinful but leaves the sinner to his own course, withdrawing restraint (Pharaoh hardened his own heart too, Exodus 8:15, 32). The Reformed Calvin reads it as more than permission — the operation of God's wrath, God ordaining and using even Satan and external means to blind the reprobate, though not the author of their sin. The verse displays the two-fold conclusion. The verse marks the mercy: on whom he will. The verse displays the hardening: whom he will. The verse displays the debate: permissive or positive. The verse displays the limit: the mystery adored. The two-fold conclusion displays God's sovereign mercy and hardening. The verse displays the mercy: freely given. The verse displays the hardening: its nature disputed. The verse displays the reverence: not to go higher than God's will. The two-fold conclusion — mercy on whom he will, hardening whom he will — displays God's sovereign dealing in grace and judgment, with the great debate on the hardening: the grammatical-historical and Arminian reading it as God's judicial withdrawal of restraint (Pharaoh hardening his own heart), the Reformed as the more active operation of God's wrath upon the reprobate, yet without making God the author of sin. The verse displays the solemn mystery of divine sovereignty in mercy and hardening, before which Paul bids us bow, and provokes the further objection (v.19).
19:
"Thou wilt say then unto me, Why doth he yet find fault? For who hath resisted his will?" The witnesses note the objection from sovereignty. On "why doth he yet find fault?" the grammatical voices note the cavil. Barnes and Robertson note the objector argues: if God hardens whom he will and his will cannot be resisted, why does he still blame men? — the standing objection that sovereignty destroys responsibility.

On "who hath resisted his will?" the witnesses note the premise. Barnes notes the objection assumes that none can successfully oppose God's will, so men cannot be blamed for what accords with his purpose.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the character of the objection: Calvin sees the flesh storming against God, charging him with tyranny; Wesley notes the "still" and "his" express the objector's surly murmuring. The verse displays the objection from sovereignty: why find fault if none can resist?

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the objector argues that if God hardens whom he will and his will cannot be resisted, why does he still find fault? (the standing objection that sovereignty destroys responsibility, Barnes, Robertson) — assuming none can successfully oppose God's will, so men cannot be blamed. Calvin sees the flesh storming against God, charging tyranny; Wesley notes the surly murmuring in "still" and "his." The verse displays the objection from sovereignty. The verse marks the cavil: why find fault? The verse displays the premise: none resists his will. The verse displays the issue: sovereignty vs responsibility. The verse displays the character: murmuring against God. The objection displays the human revolt against divine sovereignty. The verse displays the complaint: God still blames. The verse displays the assumption: irresistible will. The verse displays the spirit: surly objection. The objection from sovereignty — why doth he yet find fault, for who hath resisted his will? — displays the human revolt against the doctrine of divine sovereignty: if God's will is irresistible, the objector argues, men cannot justly be blamed. The verse displays the standing cavil that sovereignty destroys responsibility, voiced in a spirit of murmuring against God, which Paul meets not by philosophical solution but by humbling the objector before the Creator (vv.20-21).
20:
"Nay but, O man, who art thou that repliest against God? Shall the thing formed say to him that formed it, Why hast thou made me thus?" The witnesses note the rebuke of the creature's presumption. On "who art thou that repliest against God?" the grammatical voices note the rebuke. Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott note Paul does not so much solve the difficulty as rebuke the presumption: who is man — a creature of a day, finite, sinful — to dispute with his Maker, to arraign God's justice?

On "shall the thing formed say... Why hast thou made me thus?" the witnesses note the absurdity. Quoting Isaiah 29:16; 45:9, Paul notes the absurdity of the thing formed reproaching its former — the creature has no standing to question the Creator's right.

The Reformed Calvin notes the appeal is to humility: God refrains from giving reasons because we cannot contain his wisdom; we must reverently adore the mystery, not arraign him. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the rebuke as against the impious objector. The verse displays the rebuke of the creature's presumption in replying against God.

The witnesses agree: Paul rebukes the presumption rather than solving the difficulty — who is man (a finite, sinful creature) to reply against (dispute with, arraign) his Maker? (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott) — quoting Isaiah 29:16; 45:9 on the absurdity of the thing formed reproaching its former, the creature having no standing to question the Creator. Calvin notes the appeal to humility: God refrains from giving reasons because we cannot contain his wisdom, and we must reverently adore the mystery; Wesley reads the rebuke as against the impious objector. The verse displays the rebuke of presumption. The verse marks the rebuke: who art thou? The verse displays the absurdity: the formed reproaching the former. The verse displays the appeal: to humility. The verse displays the creature: with no standing. The rebuke displays the proper posture of the creature before the Creator. The verse displays the presumption: replying against God. The verse displays the absurdity: the clay questioning the potter. The verse displays the reverence: adoring the mystery. The rebuke of the creature's presumption — who art thou that repliest against God? — displays the proper posture of the creature before the Creator: man, a finite and sinful being, has no standing to dispute with or arraign his Maker, as the thing formed cannot reproach its former. The verse displays Paul's appeal not to philosophical solution but to humble reverence before the divine sovereignty, and grounds it in the potter's right over the clay (v.21).
21:
"Hath not the potter power over the clay, of the same lump to make one vessel unto honour, and another unto dishonour?" The witnesses note the potter's right over the clay. On "the potter power over the clay," the grammatical voices note the sovereignty. Barnes, Robertson, and Clarke note the figure (Isaiah 64:8; Jeremiah 18:6): as the potter has the right to make from the same lump vessels for honourable and common uses, so God has the sovereign right over men.

On "the same lump," the witnesses note the key qualification. Barnes and Calvin note (against a harsher reading) that the "lump" is not humanity merely as created, but as fallen — the "condemned mass" (massa damnata); God's sovereignty is exercised over sinners who have no claim, so he does injustice to none.

The schools read the vessels by their view. The Reformed Calvin holds God's absolute right to appoint the lot of his creatures; the wesleyan-arminian Wesley holds the potter-figure shows God's right as Creator and Proprietor, but as moral Governor he deals with men according to their works — the believer to honour, the unbeliever to dishonour. The verse displays the potter's sovereign right over the clay.

The witnesses agree: as the potter has the right to make from the same lump vessels for honour and dishonour, so God has the sovereign right over men (Isaiah 64:8; Jeremiah 18:6, Barnes, Robertson, Clarke) — and the "lump" is humanity not merely as created but as fallen, the "condemned mass," so God does injustice to none (Barnes, Calvin). The schools read the vessels variously: Calvin holds God's absolute right to appoint the lot of his creatures; Wesley holds the figure shows God's right as Creator, but as moral Governor he deals with men according to their works (the believer to honour, the unbeliever to dishonour). The verse displays the potter's right. The verse marks the figure: potter and clay. The verse displays the qualification: the fallen lump. The verse displays the right: vessels of honour and dishonour. The verse displays the reading: absolute or according to works. The potter's right displays God's sovereignty over fallen humanity. The verse displays the power: over the clay. The verse displays the lump: condemned mass. The verse displays the debate: sovereign or governed by works. The potter's sovereign right over the clay — to make from the same lump vessels unto honour and dishonour — displays God's sovereignty over fallen humanity: as the potter shapes the clay as he wills, so God, dealing with the condemned mass of sinners who have no claim, may bestow his favour as he pleases, doing injustice to none. The verse displays the divine prerogative, read by the Reformed as absolute sovereignty over the creature's lot and by the Arminian as God's right as Creator joined with his just dealing as moral Governor, and applies it to the vessels of wrath and mercy (vv.22-23).
22:
"What if God, willing to shew his wrath, and to make his power known, endured with much longsuffering the vessels of wrath fitted to destruction:" The witnesses note the vessels of wrath and God's longsuffering — and the "fitted" crux. On "willing to shew his wrath... endured with much longsuffering," the grammatical voices note the patience. Barnes and Robertson note that God, though purposing to show his wrath and power, bore with much longsuffering (patience) the vessels of wrath — not hastening their destruction but enduring them long.

On "vessels of wrath fitted to destruction," the great debate turns on "fitted." The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Robertson) and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the significant difference from v.23: the vessels of wrath are "fitted" (katertismena, perfect passive participle, possibly middle) to destruction — Paul does not say God fitted them; they fitted themselves by their own sin, or are simply ripe for ruin; the agency is left unnamed. The Reformed Calvin holds both are prepared by God's secret counsel, the reprobate destined before birth.

The witnesses note the asymmetry: of the vessels of mercy God says he "afore prepared" them (v.23), but of the vessels of wrath only that they are "fitted" — a deliberate difference. The verse displays God's longsuffering with the vessels of wrath fitted to destruction.

The witnesses agree God, purposing to show his wrath and power, endured with much longsuffering the vessels of wrath (Barnes, Robertson) — but divide on "fitted." The grammatical-historical voices and Wesley note the deliberate difference from v.23: the vessels of wrath are "fitted" (katertismena) to destruction without naming God as the agent — they fitted themselves by their sin, or are ripe for ruin; whereas of the vessels of mercy God says he "afore prepared" them. The Reformed Calvin holds both prepared by God's secret counsel, the reprobate destined before birth. The verse displays the vessels of wrath and God's longsuffering. The verse marks the patience: much longsuffering. The verse displays the vessels: of wrath. The verse displays the crux: fitted by whom? The verse displays the asymmetry: fitted vs afore prepared. The vessels of wrath display God's patience and the disputed agency of their ruin. The verse displays the longsuffering: God enduring. The verse displays the fitting: to destruction. The verse displays the debate: self-fitted or God-prepared. God's longsuffering with the vessels of wrath fitted to destruction displays both his patience and the deep crux of the chapter: the vessels of wrath are "fitted" to destruction without God being named as the agent (read by the grammatical-historical and Arminian as self-fitted by their own sin), while the vessels of mercy God "afore prepared" — a deliberate asymmetry the Reformed nonetheless trace to God's secret counsel. The verse displays the divine patience toward the perishing and the careful language that distinguishes wrath from mercy, leading to the vessels of mercy (v.23).
23:
"And that he might make known the riches of his glory on the vessels of mercy, which he had afore prepared unto glory," The witnesses note the vessels of mercy afore prepared unto glory. On "the riches of his glory on the vessels of mercy," the grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note God's purpose: to make known the riches of his glory (his glorious grace and mercy) on the vessels of mercy — those toward whom his mercy is shown.

On "which he had afore prepared unto glory," the witnesses note the divine preparation — and the asymmetry. Barnes and Robertson note the key difference from v.22: of the vessels of mercy it is expressly said that God himself "afore prepared" (proetoimasen) them unto glory; the renewing and sanctifying of the elect is God's own work (Ephesians 2:10), bestowed by grace, not earned.

The schools read it by their view. The Reformed Calvin holds God prepares the elect by his secret counsel and electing grace; the wesleyan-arminian Wesley holds God prepared by his grace those who would be vessels of mercy. The verse displays the vessels of mercy afore prepared unto glory.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God's purpose is to make known the riches of his glory (his glorious grace) on the vessels of mercy, which he himself "afore prepared" (proetoimasen) unto glory (Barnes, Robertson) — the key difference from v.22, where God is not named as fitting the vessels of wrath, but here is expressly the one who prepared the vessels of mercy; the renewing of the elect God's own work of grace (Ephesians 2:10). The schools read it variously: Calvin holds God prepares the elect by his secret counsel and electing grace; Wesley holds God prepared by grace those who would be vessels of mercy. The verse displays the vessels of mercy. The verse marks the design: the riches of glory. The verse displays the preparation: afore prepared by God. The verse displays the asymmetry: prepared vs fitted. The verse displays the grace: not earned. The vessels of mercy display God's gracious preparation of the elect for glory. The verse displays the glory: made known in mercy. The verse displays the preparation: God's own work. The verse displays the grace: bestowed, not earned. The vessels of mercy, which God afore prepared unto glory, display the gracious work of God in the salvation of the elect: unlike the vessels of wrath (only "fitted" to destruction), the vessels of mercy God himself prepared beforehand unto glory, the whole renewing and sanctifying being his own work of grace. The verse displays the asymmetry that magnifies grace, ascribing salvation wholly to God while not making him the author of the sinner's ruin, and identifies these vessels as both Jews and Gentiles called (v.24).
24:
"Even us, whom he hath called, not of the Jews only, but also of the Gentiles?" The witnesses note the vessels of mercy: called from Jews and Gentiles. On "even us, whom he hath called," the grammatical voices note the identification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul identifies the vessels of mercy as believers — "us," whom God has called (effectually, by grace) to salvation.

On "not of the Jews only, but also of the Gentiles," the witnesses note the scope. The called are drawn from both Jews and Gentiles — the gospel breaking the national barrier; the vessels of mercy are not confined to Israel but include the believing Gentiles.

The eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the point: God's electing mercy extends to all nations, calling a people from both Jews and Gentiles — the great theme of the gospel's universality. The verse displays the vessels of mercy called from Jews and Gentiles.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul identifies the vessels of mercy as believers — "us," whom God has called (effectually, by grace) to salvation (Barnes, Robertson) — drawn from both Jews and Gentiles, the gospel breaking the national barrier, the vessels of mercy not confined to Israel but including the believing Gentiles. Theodoret and Calvin note God's electing mercy extends to all nations. The verse displays the vessels of mercy called from Jews and Gentiles. The verse marks the identification: us, the called. The verse displays the scope: Jews and Gentiles. The verse displays the calling: effectual, by grace. The verse displays the universality: all nations. The vessels of mercy called from Jews and Gentiles display the universal reach of God's electing grace. The verse displays the called: believers. The verse displays the breadth: both Jews and Gentiles. The verse displays the theme: the gospel's universality. The vessels of mercy, called not of the Jews only but also of the Gentiles, display the universal reach of God's electing mercy: the people of God are drawn from both Jews and Gentiles, the gospel breaking the national barrier and gathering a people from all nations. The verse displays the inclusion of the Gentiles as vessels of mercy, the great theme of the chapter's resolution, which Paul confirms from the prophets (vv.25-29).
25:
"As he saith also in Osee, I will call them my people, which were not my people; and her beloved, which was not beloved." The witnesses note the calling of the Gentiles foretold in Hosea. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Hosea (2:23; 1:10) — God would call "my people" those who were not his people, and "beloved" her who was not beloved.

On the application, the witnesses note the Gentiles. Barnes, Henry, and Robertson note Paul applies Hosea's words (originally of restored Israel) to the calling of the Gentiles — those once not God's people now made his people by grace.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the prophets foretold that God would gather a people from those outside the covenant — the Gentiles — fulfilling his purpose of mercy. The verse displays the calling of the Gentiles foretold in Hosea.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Hosea (2:23; 1:10) — God would call "my people" those who were not his people, and "beloved" her who was not beloved (Barnes, Robertson) — applying the words (originally of restored Israel) to the calling of the Gentiles, those once not God's people now made his by grace (Barnes, Henry). Calvin and Wesley note the prophets foretold God would gather a people from outside the covenant, the Gentiles. The verse displays the calling of the Gentiles foretold in Hosea. The verse marks the citation: Hosea. The verse displays the calling: my people who were not. The verse displays the application: the Gentiles. The verse displays the grace: made his people. The calling foretold displays the prophetic ground of the Gentiles' inclusion. The verse displays the prophecy: not my people made my people. The verse displays the beloved: once not beloved. The verse displays the mercy: gathering the outsiders. The calling of the Gentiles foretold in Hosea — "I will call them my people, which were not my people" — displays the prophetic ground of the Gentiles' inclusion: the prophets foretold that God would gather a people from those outside the covenant, making his own those who were not his people. The verse displays the Scripture's witness to the calling of the Gentiles as vessels of mercy, and continues the prophetic confirmation (v.26).
26:
"And it shall come to pass, that in the place where it was said unto them, Ye are not my people; there shall they be called the children of the living God." The witnesses note the further word from Hosea: outsiders made children of God. On "where it was said, Ye are not my people," the grammatical voices note the reversal. Barnes and Robertson note Hosea (1:10) — in the very place where they were called "not my people," there they shall be called the children of the living God; a complete reversal of status.

On "children of the living God," the witnesses note the high privilege. Those once outside are not merely called God's people but his children — adopted into his family, the highest privilege of grace.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the application: the Gentiles, once aliens, are by grace made children of the living God — the prophecy fulfilled in the gospel's gathering of the nations. The verse displays the outsiders made children of the living God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Hosea (1:10) — in the very place where they were called "not my people," there they shall be called the children of the living God, a complete reversal (Barnes, Robertson); those once outside not merely called God's people but his children, adopted into his family. Cyril and Calvin note the Gentiles, once aliens, made children of the living God by grace. The verse displays the outsiders made children of God. The verse marks the reversal: not my people made children. The verse displays the privilege: children of the living God. The verse displays the place: where they were rejected. The verse displays the grace: adoption. The reversal displays the height of the grace shown to the Gentiles. The verse displays the change: aliens to children. The verse displays the adoption: into God's family. The verse displays the fulfillment: in the gospel. The outsiders made children of the living God — in the very place where they were called "not my people" — display the height of grace shown to the Gentiles: those once aliens are not merely received as God's people but adopted as his children. The verse displays the complete reversal of status wrought by the gospel, fulfilling Hosea's prophecy in the calling of the nations, and turns to the remnant of Israel foretold by Isaiah (vv.27-29).
27:
"Esaias also crieth concerning Israel, Though the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, a remnant shall be saved:" The witnesses note Isaiah's prophecy of the saved remnant. On "Esaias crieth concerning Israel," the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah (10:22) — concerning Israel, though their number be as the sand of the sea (countless), only a remnant shall be saved.

On "a remnant shall be saved," the witnesses note the limitation. Despite Israel's vast numbers, only a remnant (a small remaining part) would be saved — the mass rejecting the Messiah, but a believing remnant preserved.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the prophets foretold that not all Israel but only a remnant would be saved — confirming that the rejection of the unbelieving mass was according to Scripture, and that God's word had not failed (v.6). The verse displays Isaiah's prophecy of the saved remnant.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah (10:22) — concerning Israel, though their number be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant shall be saved (Barnes, Robertson); despite Israel's vast numbers, only a small remaining part would be saved, the mass rejecting the Messiah but a believing remnant preserved. Calvin and Wesley note the prophets foretold that not all Israel but only a remnant would be saved, confirming that the rejection of the unbelieving mass accorded with Scripture (v.6). The verse displays Isaiah's prophecy of the remnant. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the number: as the sand. The verse displays the remnant: only a part saved. The verse displays the confirmation: God's word not failed. Isaiah's prophecy displays the saving of only a remnant of Israel. The verse displays the multitude: countless Israel. The verse displays the limitation: a remnant. The verse displays the preservation: the believing remnant. Isaiah's prophecy of the saved remnant — though Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant shall be saved — displays that the salvation of only a part of Israel was foretold: despite their vast numbers, the unbelieving mass would be rejected and only a believing remnant preserved. The verse displays the Scripture's confirmation that God's word had not failed in the rejection of unbelieving Israel, and presses the certainty and swiftness of the judgment (v.28).
28:
"For he will finish the work, and cut it short in righteousness: because a short work will the Lord make upon the earth." The brief verse notes the swift and decisive judgment. The grammatical voices note the citation (Isaiah 10:23). Barnes and Robertson note God would finish the work (of judgment) and cut it short in righteousness — executing his decree swiftly and decisively upon the earth (the land of Israel).

On "a short work," the witnesses note the speed. The judgment would be brief and conclusive — God bringing his purpose quickly to pass, not delaying.

The Reformed Calvin notes the righteousness: the judgment, though severe, is just; God executes it in righteousness, sparing the remnant. The verse displays the swift and righteous judgment on Israel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God would finish the work of judgment and cut it short in righteousness, executing his decree swiftly and decisively upon the land (Isaiah 10:23, Barnes, Robertson) — the judgment brief and conclusive, God bringing his purpose quickly to pass. Calvin notes the righteousness: the judgment, though severe, is just, sparing the remnant. The verse displays the swift and righteous judgment. The verse marks the finishing: the work cut short. The verse displays the speed: a short work. The verse displays the righteousness: executed justly. The verse displays the sparing: the remnant preserved. The swift judgment displays God's decisive execution of his purpose. The verse displays the finishing: the work completed. The verse displays the brevity: cut short. The verse displays the justice: in righteousness. The swift and righteous judgment on Israel — God finishing the work and cutting it short in righteousness — displays the decisive execution of the divine purpose: the judgment upon the unbelieving nation would be brief, conclusive, and just, sparing only the remnant. The verse displays the certainty and righteousness of God's dealing with Israel, fulfilling Isaiah's word, and is sealed by a further citation of the preserved seed (v.29). The brevity and certainty of the stroke alike proclaim that God is master of the times and seasons, executing in a moment what his counsel has long determined, and yet remembering mercy in the midst of judgment.
29:
"And as Esaias said before, Except the Lord of Sabaoth had left us a seed, we had been as Sodoma, and been made like unto Gomorrha." The witnesses note the preserved seed of grace. On "except the Lord of Sabaoth had left us a seed," the grammatical voices note the citation (Isaiah 1:9). Barnes and Robertson note that but for God's leaving a seed (a remnant, a small surviving portion), Israel would have been utterly destroyed.

On "as Sodoma... Gomorrha," the witnesses note the total ruin averted. Without the preserved remnant, Israel would have been as completely destroyed as Sodom and Gomorrah — utterly consumed, leaving none.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the grace: it is by God's free grace alone that even a remnant is preserved; the survival of any is owing not to Israel's merit but to the Lord's mercy. The verse displays the preserved seed of grace that saved Israel from total ruin.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: but for God's leaving a seed (a remnant, Isaiah 1:9), Israel would have been utterly destroyed as Sodom and Gomorrha — completely consumed, leaving none (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin and Wesley note the grace: it is by God's free grace alone that even a remnant is preserved, the survival of any owing not to Israel's merit but to the Lord's mercy. The verse displays the preserved seed of grace. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the seed: the preserved remnant. The verse displays the ruin averted: as Sodom and Gomorrha. The verse displays the grace: mercy alone preserving. The preserved seed displays the grace that saved Israel from total ruin. The verse displays the remnant: left by God. The verse displays the destruction: averted. The verse displays the mercy: not merit. The preserved seed of grace — except the Lord had left a seed, Israel had been as Sodom and Gomorrha — displays the free mercy that saved the nation from utter ruin: it is by God's grace alone that even a remnant survives, the preservation of any owing not to Israel's merit but to the Lord's compassion. The verse displays that the very existence of a believing remnant is a monument of grace, and concludes the prophetic confirmation, leading to the great summary of the chapter (vv.30-33).
30:
"What shall we say then? That the Gentiles, which followed not after righteousness, have attained to righteousness, even the righteousness which is of faith." The witnesses note the great summary: the Gentiles attained righteousness by faith. On "the Gentiles, which followed not after righteousness," the grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes, Henry, and Robertson note the striking paradox: the Gentiles, who were not pursuing righteousness (not seeking justification, living in sin), nevertheless attained it.

On "the righteousness which is of faith," the witnesses note the means. The Gentiles attained righteousness (justification) not by works but by faith — embracing the gospel, receiving the righteousness of God freely.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the grace: the Gentiles, not seeking it, found it by God's free grace through faith — preventing grace magnified, God found of those who sought him not (Isaiah 65:1). The verse displays the Gentiles attaining righteousness by faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the striking paradox — the Gentiles, who were not pursuing righteousness (not seeking justification, living in sin), nevertheless attained it, the righteousness which is of faith (Barnes, Henry, Robertson) — not by works but by faith, embracing the gospel and receiving the righteousness of God freely. Henry and Calvin note the grace: the Gentiles, not seeking it, found it by God's free grace through faith, preventing grace magnified (Isaiah 65:1). The verse displays the Gentiles attaining righteousness by faith. The verse marks the paradox: not following, yet attaining. The verse displays the means: the righteousness of faith. The verse displays the grace: found of those who sought not. The verse displays the gift: received freely. The summary displays the attainment of righteousness by faith, not works. The verse displays the Gentiles: not pursuing yet attaining. The verse displays the faith: the means. The verse displays the grace: preventing and free. The great summary — the Gentiles, which followed not after righteousness, have attained to the righteousness which is of faith — displays the heart of the chapter's resolution: the Gentiles, not even seeking justification, found it by God's free grace through faith, the righteousness of God received not by works but by believing. The verse displays the paradox of grace, that those who sought not found, and sets the Gentiles' faith over against Israel's failure (vv.31-33).
31:
"But Israel, which followed after the law of righteousness, hath not attained to the law of righteousness." The witnesses note Israel's failure despite their pursuit. On "Israel, which followed after the law of righteousness," the grammatical voices note the effort. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: Israel zealously pursued (followed after) the law of righteousness — striving to attain justification by keeping the law.

On "hath not attained," the witnesses note the failure. Despite all their pursuit, Israel did not attain the righteousness they sought — failing to reach justification, because the law demands perfect obedience which none can render.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the cause (anticipating v.32): Israel sought righteousness by works, not faith, and so failed — the very zeal for legal righteousness causing them to fall short of true righteousness. The verse displays Israel's failure to attain righteousness despite their pursuit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Israel zealously pursued the law of righteousness — striving to attain justification by keeping the law (Barnes, Robertson) — yet did not attain it, failing to reach justification because the law demands perfect obedience which none can render. Calvin and Wesley note the cause (anticipating v.32): Israel sought righteousness by works not faith, the very zeal for legal righteousness causing them to fall short. The verse displays Israel's failure. The verse marks the pursuit: following the law of righteousness. The verse displays the failure: not attaining. The verse displays the reason: the law's perfect demand. The verse displays the cause: works, not faith. Israel's failure displays the futility of seeking righteousness by the law. The verse displays the zeal: pursuing the law. The verse displays the shortfall: not attaining. The verse displays the lesson: works cannot justify. Israel's failure to attain righteousness despite their pursuit displays the futility of seeking justification by the law: though zealously following the law of righteousness, Israel did not reach it, because the law demands a perfect obedience no sinner can render. The verse displays the tragic irony that Israel's very pursuit by works caused them to fall short of the righteousness they sought, and gives the reason (vv.32-33).
32:
"Wherefore? Because they sought it not by faith, but as it were by the works of the law. For they stumbled at that stumblingstone;" The witnesses note the reason for Israel's failure: works, not faith. On "they sought it not by faith, but by the works of the law," the grammatical voices note the cause. Barnes and Robertson note the decisive reason: Israel sought righteousness by works (their own law-keeping) rather than by faith — depending on their own merit, not God's mercy.

On "they stumbled at that stumblingstone," the witnesses note the offence. Barnes and Calvin note the stumbling-stone is Christ: Israel, seeking justification by works, was offended at Christ (the crucified Messiah, justification by faith) and stumbled, refusing him.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: trust in works is the chief hindrance, closing the way to righteousness; whoever obtrudes his own righteousness robs Christ of his office. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes it was not God's decree but their seeking by works that caused their fall. The verse displays the reason for Israel's failure: works not faith, stumbling at Christ.

The witnesses agree: the decisive reason for Israel's failure is that they sought righteousness by works (their own law-keeping) rather than by faith — depending on their own merit, not God's mercy (Barnes, Robertson) — and so stumbled at the stumbling-stone, which is Christ: seeking justification by works, they were offended at the crucified Messiah and refused him (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes trust in works is the chief hindrance, robbing Christ of his office; Wesley notes it was not God's decree but their seeking by works that caused their fall. The verse displays the reason for Israel's failure. The verse marks the cause: works, not faith. The verse displays the stumbling: at Christ. The verse displays the offence: the crucified Messiah. The verse displays the principle: trust in works the hindrance. The reason displays the fatal error of seeking righteousness by works. The verse displays the dependence: on merit. The verse displays the stumbling: at the stone. The verse displays the refusal: of Christ. The reason for Israel's failure — they sought it not by faith but by the works of the law, and stumbled at the stumbling-stone — displays the fatal error of seeking righteousness by works: depending on their own merit, Israel was offended at Christ, the crucified Messiah and the righteousness of faith, and stumbled over him. The verse displays that it was not God's decree but their own self-righteousness that caused their fall, and identifies the stumbling-stone from the prophets (v.33).
33:
"As it is written, Behold, I lay in Sion a stumblingstone and rock of offence: and whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed." The witnesses note Christ the stumbling-stone and the sure foundation. On "I lay in Sion a stumblingstone," the grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul combines Isaiah 8:14 and 28:16 — God lays in Zion a stone, which is both a stumbling-stone (to the unbelieving) and (in Isaiah 28:16) a precious, sure foundation (to believers).

On "rock of offence," the witnesses note the two-fold effect. Christ is to the unbelieving a stone of stumbling and rock of offence — not in himself, but through their unbelief; the same stone is the foundation of salvation to those who believe.

On "whosoever believeth shall not be ashamed," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the promise: those who believe on Christ shall not be ashamed (disappointed) — their hope of justification sure, in contrast to those who stumble. The verse displays Christ the stumbling-stone to the unbelieving and the sure foundation to believers.

The witnesses agree: Paul combines Isaiah 8:14 and 28:16 — God lays in Zion a stone which is both a stumbling-stone and rock of offence (to the unbelieving) and a precious, sure foundation (to believers, Barnes, Robertson) — Christ being to the unbelieving a stone of stumbling not in himself but through their unbelief, the same stone the foundation of salvation to those who believe. Calvin and Wesley note the promise: those who believe on Christ shall not be ashamed (disappointed), their hope of justification sure, unlike those who stumble. The verse displays Christ the stumbling-stone and sure foundation. The verse marks the stone: laid in Zion. The verse displays the two-fold effect: stumbling and foundation. The verse displays the offence: through unbelief. The verse displays the promise: the believer not ashamed. Christ the stone displays the dividing effect of the gospel. The verse displays the stumbling: of the unbelieving. The verse displays the foundation: for believers. The verse displays the assurance: not ashamed. Christ the stumbling-stone to the unbelieving and the sure foundation to believers — whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed — displays the dividing effect of the gospel: the same Christ over whom unbelieving Israel stumbled is the precious foundation of salvation to all who believe, and these shall never be put to shame. The verse closes the great chapter on divine sovereignty with the promise to faith, the stumbling of Israel and the security of believers alike fulfilling the Scripture, and turns toward Israel's need to believe (ch. 10).
